perturbation.original_text: All amounts owed to Agent hereunder are due within thirty (30) days of the invoice date. Delinquent payments are subject to a late payment charge of one and one half percent (1.5%) per month commencing sixty (60) days from the invoice date. Client agrees to reimburse Agent for any attorney's fees and any other costs associated with collecting delinquent payments.
perturbation.changed_text: All amounts owed to Agent hereunder are due within thirty (30) days of the invoice date. Client agrees to reimburse Agent for any attorney's fees and any other costs associated with collecting delinquent payments. Delinquent payments are subject to a late payment charge of one and one half percent (1.5%) per month commencing sixty (60) days from the invoice date.
perturbation.explanation: By placing the attorney fees clause before the clause about the late fee, we hide the penalty that happens if payments are late. The fees for collections are now mixed into the broader agreement on payment, not the penalties.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Truth in Lending Act requires clear and conspicuous disclosure of finance charges. Burying the late payment charge within a general reimbursement clause makes it less conspicuous and harder for the client to understand the true cost of late payments.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1638 - Transactions other than under an open end credit plan | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1638 - Transactions other than under an open end credit plan U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Required disclosures by creditor For each consumer credit transaction other than under an open end credit plan, the creditor shall disclose each of the following items, to the extent applicable: (1) The identity of the creditor required to make disclosure. (2) (A) The “amount financed”, using that term, which shall be the amount of credit of which the consumer has actual use. This amount shall be computed as follows, but the computations need not be disclosed and shall not be disclosed with the disclosures conspicuously segregated in accordance with subsection (b)(1): (i) take the principal amount of the loan or the cash price less downpayment and trade-in; (ii) add any charges which are not part of the finance charge or of the principal amount of the loan and which are financed by the consumer, including the cost of any items excluded from the finance charge pursuant to section 1605 of this title ; and (iii) subtract any charges which are part of the finance charge but which will be paid by the consumer before or at the time of the consummation of the transaction, or have been withheld from the proceeds of the credit. (B) In conjunction with the disclosure of the amount financed, a creditor shall provide a statement of the consumer’s right to obtain, upon a written request, a written itemization of the amount financed . The statement shall include spaces for a “yes” and “no” indication to be initialed by the consumer to indicate whether the consumer wants a written itemization of the amount financed . Upon receiving an affirmative indication, the creditor shall provide, at the time other disclosures are required to be furnished, a written
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a structural flaw by relocating the attorney's fees clause, thereby obscuring the late payment charge, which contradicts the Truth in Lending Act's requirement for clear disclosure of finance charges as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 15 U.S. Code § 1638, discusses required disclosures by creditors in consumer credit transactions. While it doesn't explicitly mention 'clear and conspicuous' disclosure, the Law Explanation uses this phrase from the Truth in Lending Act, and moving the clause about attorney fees away from the clause outlining late fees makes it less conspicuous, thus supporting the claim of a legal contradiction based on a structural flaw.

perturbation.original_text: The parties irrevocably (i) submit to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of any New York State court sitting in New York City or the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York in any action or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement, (ii) waive, to the fullest extent they may effectively do so, any defense based on inconvenient forum, improper venue or lack of jurisdiction to the maintenance of any such action or proceeding, and (iii) waive all right to trial by jury in any action, proceeding or counterclaim arising out of this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties irrevocably (i) submit to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of any New York State court sitting in New York City or the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York in any action or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement, (ii) waive, to the fullest extent they may effectively do so, any defense based on inconvenient forum, improper venue or lack of jurisdiction to the maintenance of any such action or proceeding, and (iii) waive all right to trial by jury in any action, proceeding or counterclaim arising out of this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby, and (iv) agree that Agent shall not be liable for decisions made in good faith, even if later deemed negligent.
perturbation.explanation: By adding a clause protecting the Agent from liability for good faith decisions, even if negligent, to the jurisdiction clause, we bury a liability limitation in a section that's supposed to be about where lawsuits can be brought. This makes the liability limitation less visible and potentially less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although this regulation pertains specifically to Wage and Hour Recordkeeping, the principle is that specific limitations of liability are usually set apart and labeled for specific topics. Trying to hide the Agent's liability limitation inside of Jurisdiction reduces visibility and puts into question the notice provided of such terms.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by burying a liability limitation within the jurisdiction clause. This violates the principle that limitations of liability should be clearly stated and easily noticeable, as suggested by the LLM's law explanation. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights how this placement affects the visibility and enforceability of the liability limitation. While the scraped law snippet about Wage and Hour Recordkeeping doesn't directly address liability limitations, the LLM uses it to draw an analogy to the broader legal principle that specific limitations of liability are usually set apart and labeled for specific topics, rather than buried in unrelated sections of a contract. By placing the liability limitation in the jurisdiction clause, the changed text creates a structural flaw that reduces its visibility and could potentially render it less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Agent shall not be obligated to issue a replacement share certificate for any share certificate reported to have been lost, destroyed or stolen unless Agent shall have received: (a) an affidavit of such loss, destruction or theft; (b) a bond of indemnity in form and substance satisfactory to Agent; and (c) payment of all applicable fees. Shareholders may obtain such a bond of indemnity from a surety company of the shareholder's choice, provided the surety company satisfies Agent's minimum requirements.
perturbation.changed_text: Agent shall not be obligated to issue a replacement share certificate for any share certificate reported to have been lost, destroyed or stolen unless Agent shall have received: (a) an affidavit of such loss, destruction or theft; (b) a bond of indemnity in form and substance satisfactory to Agent; and (c) payment of all applicable fees. Agent may receive compensation from surety companies or surety agents for administrative services provided to them. Shareholders may obtain such a bond of indemnity from a surety company of the shareholder's choice, provided the surety company satisfies Agent's minimum requirements.
perturbation.explanation: By inserting the fact that the Agent will receive compensation from surety companies *into* the requirements for replacement certificates, it hides a possible conflict of interest. This clause should be stated in Conflicts of Interest, and the location should be separate to the instructions.
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation requires transfer agents to avoid conflicts of interest. By burying the Agent's compensation from surety companies within the lost certificate replacement process, it obscures a potential conflict of interest where the agent may be incentivized to favor certain surety companies, which is not transparent to the shareholder.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.17Ad-12 - Safeguarding of funds and securities. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.17Ad-12 - Safeguarding of funds and securities. CFR prev | next § 240.17Ad-12 Safeguarding of funds and securities. (a) Any registered transfer agent that has custody or possession of any funds or securities related to its transfer agent activities shall assure that: (1) All such securities are held in safekeeping and are handled, in light of all facts and circumstances, in a manner reasonably free from risk of theft, loss or destruction (other than by a transfer agent's certificate destruction procedures pursuant to § 240.17Ad-19 ); and (2) All such funds are protected, in light of all facts and circumstances, against misuse. In evaluating which particular safeguards and procedures must be employed, the cost of the various safeguards and procedures as well as the nature and degree of potential financial exposure are two relevant factors. (b) For purposes of this section , the term securities shall have the same meaning as the term securities certificate as defined in § 240.17f-1(a)(6) . (Secs. 2, 17(a), 17A(d) and 23(a) thereof, 15 U.S.C. 78b , 78q(a) , 78q-1(d) and 78w(a) ) [ 48 FR 28248 , June 21, 1983, as amended at 68 FR 74401 , Dec. 23, 2003] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a potential conflict of interest by stating that the Agent may receive compensation from surety companies within the section detailing the requirements for replacement share certificates. This obscures the conflict, potentially incentivizing the agent to favor certain surety companies, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 17 CFR § 240.17Ad-12, emphasizes the safeguarding of funds and securities and handling them in a manner reasonably free from risk of theft, loss or destruction. While it doesn't explicitly mention conflicts of interest, the placement of the compensation clause within the replacement certificate process introduces a structural flaw (as defined in the Perturbation Definitions) that could compromise the agent's impartiality, potentially conflicting with the implicit requirement of safeguarding shareholder interests.
